T H E B I G I D E A
What Is a Controller Area Network?
3
■Robust serial bus. Lets microcontrollers and ECUs talk to each other
directly, with no host computer.
■Message-based, not address-based. Nodes broadcast messages
tagged by an identifier; every node decides what to accept.
■Multi-master. Any node may start transmitting whenever the bus is idle.
■Just two wires. CAN_H and CAN_L carry all traffic, slashing wiring
weight and cost.
■Differential & half-duplex. Differential signalling gives excellent
immunity to electrical noise.
2
wires carry the entire network
1 Mbit/s
maximum classic CAN bit rate
100+
ECUs across multiple CAN domains
1986
introduced by Bosch at SAE
O R I G I N S & STA N D A R D S
History & Standardization
4
Four decades from a Bosch research project to a family of ISO standards.
1983
Bosch starts
CAN
development
1986
Unveiled at SAE
Congress
1991
CAN 2.0 A/B;
first in a car
1993
ISO 11898
published
2012
CAN FD
released by
Bosch
2015
ISO 11898-1
adds CAN FD
2024
ISO 11898-1
Ed.3; CAN XL
standardised
CAN in Automation (CiA) — the international users' and manufacturers' group — was founded in 1992 to steward the technology.
VA L U E P R O P O S I T I O N
Why Engineers Choose CAN
5
Low cost & light wiring
A single twisted pair replaces bulky
point-to-point looms.
Noise immunity
Differential signalling rejects common-
mode interference.
Built-in error handling
Five detection mechanisms plus
automatic fault confinement.
Deterministic priority
Bitwise arbitration guarantees the most
urgent message wins.
Truly multi-master
Nodes can be added or removed
without redesigning the bus.
Mature ecosystem
Decades of tools, silicon and higher-
layer standards.
C A N I N T H E V E H I C L E
A CAN Network Inside the Car
ECUs cluster into domains, bridged by a gateway across low- and high-speed CAN buses.
C A N I N T H E V E H I C L E
CAN Bus Backbone
ECUs branch off two terminated CAN buses, bridged by a central gateway.
120 Ω 120 Ω
120 Ω 120 Ω
Gateway
High-speed CAN bus
Low-speed CAN bus
Engine control Transmission ABS SRS EBA Oil supply
Radio GPS Infotainment Door control Light control Climate control
ECU Electronic Control Unit ABS Anti-lock Braking System SRS Supplemental Restraint System EBA Emergency Brake Assist GPS Global Positioning System
High-speed CAN Low-speed CAN Gateway
B E F O R E & A F T E R C A N
From Wiring Harness to Two-Wire Bus
One twisted pair replaces the tangle — less weight, lower cost, easier service.
6
BEFORE — POINT-TO-POINT WIRING AFTER — TWO-WIRE CAN BUS
Dozens of dedicated wires — heavy looms, higher cost, hard to service. One twisted pair links every ECU — lighter, cheaper, robust.
N E T W O R K S T R U C T U R E
Architecture & Bus Topology
7
■Linear bus. Nodes tap onto one shared two-wire backbone via
short stubs.
■Each node has three parts. A microcontroller, a CAN controller
and a CAN transceiver.
■Terminated at both ends. A 120 Ω resistor at each end matches
the cable impedance and kills reflections.
■Scalable. Classic high-speed CAN supports up to ~32 nodes
per segment; more with modern transceivers.
Each node: a microcontroller, CAN controller and transceiver
Inside one CAN node — three building blocks
Micro-controller CAN Controller CAN Transceiver
CAN_H
CAN_L
CAN_H
CAN_L
120 Ω
ECU 1 ECU 2 ECU 3
120 Ω
Twisted pair + 120 Ω termination CAN_H
CAN_L
120 Ω
Wires twist to cancel noise; a 120 Ω resistor closes each end.
